summary: Joe Reis presents survey data showing that 95% of data practitioners identify training, ownership, time, and standards—not better tooling—as their biggest data modeling pain points. He argues that AI amplifies both competence and incompetence, making organizational fundamentals like named owners and enforced standards more critical than ever.
key_points: Only 4.8% of practitioners said better tooling would improve data modeling, while 95.2% pointed to training, requirements, time, and ownership
Teams with formal modeling standards report models holding up well roughly five times as often as teams with loose or no standards
82% of practitioners use AI tools daily, but AI pointed at a broken foundation generates broken systems at unprecedented velocity
Half the field works in environments where data modeling has no real owner, with 42.5% saying modeling decisions are made by whoever builds the pipeline
money_quotes: AI amplifies what you know about and what you're good at. AI also amplifies what you don't know about and what you're bad at.
The ungoverned path has no bottleneck, so that's where models get built.
Speed works if you know how to maneuver. Speed works against you when you don't know what you're doing. Speed kills if you're acting like an amateur.
This is the equivalent of an untrained runner buying fancy sub-2 hour marathon shoes, expecting to be a professional runner. The hard work is the hard work. Period.
The same forcing function pushing leadership to skip modeling is the one that's going to make them regret it.
key_insight: The data modeling crisis is fundamentally an organizational ownership problem, not a tooling problem, and AI adoption without fixing these foundations will only accelerate the generation of broken systems.
